content:
7 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

I used to have a Notes app full of half-finished ChatGPT prompts. You know the type — "draft an email about..." typed in a hurry, abandoned mid-sentence, buried under grocery lists and Wi-Fi passwords. It took me embarrassingly long to realize the problem wasn't ChatGPT. It was that I never wrote prompts worth saving in the first place.
Somewhere in the last year, that changed. I started treating my best prompts the way a chef treats a good recipe — write it once, refine it, keep it in a place you'll actually find again. And the difference showed up fast. Not in some dramatic "AI transformed my career" way, but in smaller, more honest ways: fewer late nights rewriting the same email three times, faster meeting notes, less staring at a blank doc wondering how to start.
This isn't a list of clever tricks or prompt-engineering wizardry. It's ten prompts I keep coming back to — the ones that quietly do more work than they look like they should. Steal them, tweak them, make them yours.
1. The "Say It Like I Mean It" Email Prompt
Most of us don't struggle to write emails. We struggle to write them without sounding either too stiff or too apologetic.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

"Rewrite this email so it sounds confident and direct, but still warm. Keep it under 120 words. Here’s the draft: [paste your rough draft]"
What I like about this one is the constraint — under 120 words forces ChatGPT to cut the throat-clearing. No "I hope this finds you well." No three paragraphs before the actual ask. Just the point, delivered like someone who respects your time and theirs.
2. The Meeting Notes Prompt That Doesn’t Sound Like a Robot Wrote It
Raw meeting notes are chaos. Everyone knows this. But turning that chaos into something your team will actually read is where most of us give up halfway through.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

"Turn these rough meeting notes into a clean summary with three sections: Decisions Made, Action Items (with owners), and Open Questions. Notes: [paste notes]"
The magic isn't the formatting — it's the separation of decisions from open questions. Half the confusion after meetings comes from people not knowing which is which. This prompt forces that clarity before it ever leaves your screen.
3. The Devil’s Advocate Prompt
Here's a habit worth building: before you commit to a decision, ask ChatGPT to argue against it.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

"I’m planning to [describe your decision]. Play devil’s advocate and give me the three strongest arguments against this, as if you were a skeptical colleague who wants me to succeed but isn’t afraid to challenge me."
I use this before pitching ideas, sending proposals, even before quitting jobs (once). It doesn't replace your own judgment. But it catches blind spots — the ones you can't see because you're too close to your own idea.
4. The "Explain This Like I’m Smart, Not Simple" Prompt
There's a difference between simplifying something and dumbing it down. Most explanations online do the second one, and it's insulting if you're not actually a beginner.
"Explain [topic] to me the way you’d explain it to a smart professional who’s new to this specific area — assume general intelligence, just not domain knowledge."
This single line changes everything about the response quality. You stop getting kindergarten metaphors and start getting explanations that respect your intelligence while still filling the actual gap.
5. The Difficult Conversation Rehearsal Prompt
Nobody enjoys telling a client their timeline slipped, or giving a teammate feedback that stings a little. But avoiding it usually makes things worse.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

"I need to have a difficult conversation about [situation]. Role-play as the other person and let me practice what I want to say. React realistically, including some pushback."
This one feels a little strange the first time you try it. Then it becomes one of the most useful prompts on this entire list. Rehearsing out loud — even with an AI — changes how steady you sound when the real conversation happens.
6. The Resume Line Sharpener
Most resume bullets are either bragging without proof or facts without impact. Neither works.
"Rewrite this resume bullet to lead with the result, include a number if possible, and cut any filler words. Bullet: [paste your line]"
I've rebuilt more resumes than I can count — my own, friends', colleagues asking for a quick look. This prompt alone fixes 80 percent of what makes resumes forgettable. Numbers aren't decoration; they're proof.
7. The Weekly Priority Reset
Some Mondays, everything feels urgent. Which usually means nothing actually is.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

"Here’s my task list for this week: [paste list]. Help me sort these into Must Do, Should Do, and Could Skip, based on impact versus effort."
It's a small prompt, but it does something bigger than task management — it forces you to be honest about what actually matters versus what just feels loud.
8. The "Translate My Rambling Into a Clear Ask" Prompt
We've all sent a message that took four paragraphs to ask a question that needed one sentence.
"Here’s my rambling explanation of what I need. Turn it into one clear, direct message that asks for exactly what I want. [paste your ramble]"
This is the prompt I reach for right before hitting send on anything important. It's humbling how often my "clear" first draft turns out to be three ideas fighting for space.
9. The Learning Accelerator Prompt
Reading about a skill and actually being able to use it are two different things. This prompt bridges that gap.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

"Give me a 5-question quiz on [topic] that tests real understanding, not just memorization. After I answer, tell me what I got wrong and why."
I use this constantly for anything technical — new tools, frameworks, concepts I've only half-understood from articles. It turns passive reading into something closer to a conversation with a patient teacher who won't let you fake it.
10. The Honest Feedback Prompt
This one might be the most valuable, and the one people use the least, because it requires a bit of ego to set aside.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

"Read this and tell me honestly what’s weak about it. Don’t soften it for my feelings. Be specific about what to fix and why."
Most of us surround ourselves with people too polite to tell us our work has problems. ChatGPT, at least, has no reason to spare your feelings. Use that.
None of these prompts are complicated. That's sort of the point. The best prompts aren't clever — they're clear, specific, and built around a real problem you actually have. Save the ones that solve something for you. Delete the ones that don't. And keep refining, because the version of a prompt that works today probably isn't the final version.
The professionals getting the most out of ChatGPT right now aren't the ones with secret prompt libraries nobody else has access to. They're the ones who bothered to write their prompts down, tested them more than once, and actually used them when it mattered.
Start with one from this list today. See what changes.
If even one of these saved you a rewrite or a headache, hit follow — I write about AI tools and prompts every week, no fluff. Drop a comment with the prompt you’re stealing first, I read every one. Know someone drowning in email drafts? Share this with them. And if you want to keep the conversation going, connect with me on LinkedIn — I post the ones that don’t make it into these articles.
